speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Morris Kesselman, survivor. K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N. May 1st, 1996. You were telling us how you were made to dismantle the crematoria. And I had asked you about the gas chambers. And can you tell me then, what happened after you worked on the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked there several weeks, from October to-- late October to early January. And we were going every morning. We were coming in the evening. We actually didn't accomplish too much. And one morn-- the-- January 17, the block elders, which was a Jewish-- a French Jew, comes back to us inside. And he says to us, you know, the SS is gone. And they're evacuating the camp. And my suggestion is get yourself mixed up. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened. We got out. I was very good clothed. I wore a heavy Russian coat, with a Russian hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had good shoes because of the possibility of having good clothes, because they brought so much clothes in, we could have any clothes we want. And we-- I took one blanket. And some people broke open the-- the warehouses for food. And there was meat for the Germans. But I didn't take any. I found lumps of sugar. And I filled my pockets up with lumps of sugar. I still don't know why. But I figured maybe so what, I have a drink. And they marched us out, that day, to a football field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who marched you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the SS had left? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, the SS didn't left. The SS who had left what watched us, our-- our barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were still SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which watched the whole camp. And we got mixed up. And we were marching. And they bought us in, actually, to Auschwitz, Camp Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if you had not mixed in with the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably would have been killed. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact is, when we came into Mauthausen, they asked who worked in crematorium? We got good jobs for-- for everybody. Now, who's going to tell them? What happened when you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got to Auschwitz. We sort of got inside. It was very cold. We didn't get any food. But there were quite a few thousand people in Auschwitz already. And then the next morning, they lined us up again, to march further. And knowing now, that-- I think 10,000, 12,000 people got liberated in Auschwitz, from the Russians. And I'll be honest with you. If I wouldn't be afraid, I would have stayed there too. But I was afraid. And went out on a march. And I marched 28, 29 days. Some of us were-- were loaded into boxcars. I wasn't. Until I came into Mauthausen, through Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you were walking all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. And anybody who couldn't proceed, only sat down, was shot when-- right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food and water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did not-- we were not given anything. Luckily, I had sugar. And the snow on the ground kept me going. But passing by Czechoslovakia, people tried to bring us bread and water. But they were shot-- the SS shot at them. And they went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about walking through Poland? Did you see any civilians when you were going through Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, we saw some through the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter. People didn't work. We didn't walk through the cities. We saw people standing up there and making certain motions to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- was their reaction when they saw you all walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them were making, like, a knife to the throat. You know? And some just stared and watched us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very cold. Snow on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the other people dressed for the cold weather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were not. Some did. Most of them weren't. But they had blankets. They wrapped around in blankets. But they got tired of-- of dragging the blankets. So they threw them away. You walked on blankets. And as you walked, then near the ravine, there were plenty of dead people laying around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you arrived at Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think nothing. I mean, at that point, I don't know what we had. We didn't think of anything. We got brought in. It was very cold. And we were to stay, many hours, outside. Until they again, they put us into a bath, a cold shower. And-- and same thing with clothes. They took all our clothes away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they always thought what we hiding in that clothes. That we couldn't carry in any money, any pictures, or any-- [? lettuce, ?] or whatever. And we were put in a block, in a quarantine for two weeks. We sat in our underwear. Would you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little food. And finally, we were shipped up to the next camp. And the next camp was Ebensee. This was a camp-- from that camp, you didn't-- did-- nobody thought that anybody was-- that's going to be an end to it. We actually live in a mountain of steel. A mountain, a stone mountain. They dug out and we lived there. We worked there. There was no daylight. There was nothing. Luckily, they ask who was electrician. So I picked up my arm. I don't know why. And I carried around two [INAUDIBLE] on my shoulder for three months. So I didn't do any hard work. So this helped me survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did they do in the camp that was hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly they were dug in, in those mountains. And they were refining gasoline for [? water. ?] And I was heard that 250,000, 300,000 people died dugging those mountains out. Because also, the big trains were going in-- into those hole places, where they dug out. Tankers, to get the gasoline out. It wasn't just Jews. Jews was a minority then, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of nationality. Russian, Yugoslavians, Armenians, all Europeans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they paid or were they slave labor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Slave  labor. The only people who were paid were strictly German engineers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get food at Ebensee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? Even worse than Auschwitz. The closer to the end of the war, the less we got. They gave us a piece of bread. If you'd throw it against the wall, it would stick on the wall. But I always said to myself that I-- if I ever leave the camp, and if I'll die, it won't be in camp. It's going to be in a place of work. Because if you did not go to work, you would have there already, they took the shoes away amongst each other, you know. It was terrible. So I went to the last day. As much as I could work on myself, go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you going in these camps? What sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to know something? On the end, I never give up life. And on top of it, that I was a little boy. I didn't eat much food. [LAUGHS] I think. But somehow towards the end, I says, somehow I'm going to survive. We always hoped that maybe one of us will survive and tell the world what was going on. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the American and English forces were coming close?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw airplanes coming over the camps. They bombed. And many times we didn't even go into the shelters.

speaker: interviewer
text: We said, ah, what the-- what's the-- what's the sense? Let's get it over with. But knowing where the front is, aware of this, no. Because we knew that it's coming to an end. But how, exactly, we didn't know. Was there any medical care in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I was liberated, I was weighing 90-- 90-- 90-odd pounds, very skinny. And fact is, I was-- when I was liberated by the Third Army, I was six weeks in the tent hospital. And I had a touch of-- of tuberculosis. Because when I arrived in-- before I was to emigrate, my x-ray showed some symptoms of a tuberculous. And they kept me back six months to see if there's any change. And there wasn't any change. So this let me come into this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think was-- this happened in Ebensee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly when it happened. But the only x-rays from that-- I was liberated when they x-rayed us. And they put us in the hospital. I was told that I had a touch of tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that you were going to be liberated, that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till the day before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened the day before?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were at work. And we heard rumors from the civilian Germans. And everybody didn't work already. And they scattered around. And we went home. [? They were a ?] [? little ?] [? bit-- ?] And one German, a civilian German, well, he got-- he got in a rage. And he threw shovel handles at us. Because he-- we weren't all together. And then one shovel hit my nose. Until today, I'm suffering. I cannot breathe. I had an operation in 1951. And then the next day, we came in. In the morning, we're supposed to get up to work. We're not going to work. They told us that the Americans are coming in. And they want us to go in, into the mines to be protected in case there-- there's some shooting, going to be. Which actually, the mines were-- were mined [? to ?] [? blow us ?] all up. But the SS was gone. And the perimeters, what civilian people, with a little gun in their hands, 70, 80-year-old men. So the leader-- leadership says forget it. We're not going to any camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And several hours later, the unit-- the army came in. And would you believe, I couldn't get off that-- that bunk? My feet wouldn't carry me.

speaker: interviewee
text: A day before, I was stood up. But I collapsed. With joy or for else, I don't know what it was. But they took-- I, particular, for one, was taken to the hospital. And I was taken care of. But a lot of us which didn't go to the hospital and got too much food, died from the food. In the thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any way that could have been prevented?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army didn't know. They thought people, with the starvation-- so you know soldiers. They gave everything but the hat off their heads. And they-- the people who haven't eaten for months, so they weren't used to it. And this brought on the dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to get you, to take you to the-- were you taken to a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Red Cross. American Red Cross, yeah. I was put on a stretcher. And they wrote it on the side of each truck, 8 of us, 10 of us. And they brought us in. They didn't have a hospital. So they pitched a hospital. Tents. That camp was burned down to nothing. It was so infested. So they burned down the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the German guards that had been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned to you, most of the SS was gone. But civilian people-- what happened, I don't know. I mean, I wasn't out there. And I don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return to your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For more than one reason. I knew that my family's gone. And in particular, I did not like Poland, and the people in it. Because I felt if they wouldn't be-- if they would be more honest and would be nicer people, I wouldn't have to go through all those five camps. I could have lived in Germany, on that farm, and probably survived there, working, if they wouldn't give me out that I was a Jew. And I had nothing to go back to. The memories, it was too much. Especially me, with seeing those hundreds of thousands of people who were killed, and being bystander. I felt guilty, terrible guilty, that I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your camp was near a Polish city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people who lived in the city know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course they--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the killings at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. The chimneys were right there. I mean, how could-- the smell was miles away. And we were only 5 kilometer-- we're 3 and 1/2 miles from the city of Oswiecim. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If anybody tells you that they didn't know-- not that-- maybe not everybody knew. But it was a known fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you decide to go after you left the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the Jewish agencies came in. And we were separated from non-Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They opened up former military barracks, buildings. And we were placed there. They brought us a lot of food. They took care of us. But the uncertainty was there. We didn't know where to go. We didn't know what's going to happen tomorrow, or what's going to happen this afternoon. We went on from hour till hour, we're waiting for the breakfast, to the lunch, to the supper. We went out in the city. Nothing there. Everything was dead. You didn't see a German. They all had run away. And it-- it wasn't a big city. Until, maybe, two months later. The Jewish brigade from the English Army, the Bricha, came in. And they said to us, there may be a chance to get you to Israel. They didn't want to take any women. And in fact, I didn't know if women were liberated. But they told us that our women were liberated. But they want strictly men. And they told us the next morning to be in a particular spot. And they load us on trucks. And they brought us into Salzburg. I remember now, this trip. And this was sort of halfway into Italy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told us to disappear. They brought us some food. They said, disappear. And tomorrow morning, you come back here, and there and there. We'll find you. And the second day, they brought us into Italy. And they brought us into a city of Modena. Modena was a big college town. And they said, you're now in Italy. And now we're going to wait for a chance to get you further. But get off and find yourself a place. Yeah, we know you-- something, you'll find. I was traveling with three companions. We were four people, liberated together. And we weren't far from a railroad. And I said, there must be some trains or wagons around. Let's go anywhere, stay there overnight. Didn't have any luggage. We didn't have nothing with us. And we came down to that area. There was several hundreds already over there, not knowing that anybody was going to be there. And we stayed overnight. We stayed a few nights. We went out in the streets. The Italians are very nice people. And they saw who we were. We were shaved heads. And they offered us some food. And we lived on handouts. Then finally one day, they woke up. They see these boys-- there's quite a few of us. They open up the University of Modena. And several thousand came in there. And they kept us there. And we slept on tile floor. And they gave us a little food. It was no picnic. And we wait around, and no-- no answer. Finally, they breaking us up. And they took us, several hundred, into a town, Ankara, about 300, 400 miles more south. Over there were the English, occupying this particular area. And we were put in, in tents. The heat was out of this world. [LAUGHS] If you put-- step down barefoot, you burn your feet. We were given a tent, four people, with four straw sacks and blankets. And they fed us three times a day. A little soup, in the morning, some bread. The bread was made not out of wheat. It got so hard, where in five minutes, you couldn't even bite into it. And we stayed around for several weeks. And then the religious people came-- start coming in from the United States, Orthodox Jews, and bringing us Torahs and religious books, and telling us look, we got to start a new life. Whatever happened, happened. And you could not convince them. And we didn't believe much in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So another few weeks later, I said to the boys, what are we hanging around here for? There's many people in Germany. Whatever's going to happen to them will happen to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least over there, we'll have a roof over our head. We can have a little bit of food, and we're young. And they had four blankets. It's funny. [LAUGHS] When we walked in, we had a sign for the blankets. So we had a rucksack. So two guys took two-- two blankets in the rucksack. And they gave up the two blankets. They signed out, not knowing whether they had the other-- our two blankets in their rucksack. And we walked out from the camp. Because no one watched that we couldn't go in and out. And we saw those two blankets. And we got some Italian money, to buy bread. It took us two weeks to go back to Austria. From one train to the other train, to a coal train, to the mountains. And we finally came back to Germany into a camp, Feldafing, near Munich. And over there we met more people. At least we got food, and several thousand among us. Still don't know what's going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the German people treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early days, they were very much afraid of us. But then couldn't treat us bad, because they didn't have much. And if they have, they-- they hid it. They hided everything. And we weren't afraid of them. I mean, I give you example. We went out in the street and we stopped a milk truck. There's nothing moving in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we want to go west. He went east. So we asked him, where you going? He said, where you want to go? We're going to go west. He said, I'm going east. Let's go east.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had nothing to do with ourselves. So they were very much afraid of us. So we didn't care if they treated us right or wrong. We had no respect for them anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they seemed to be afraid of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they afraid of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not exactly for us, as Jews. When we first were liberated, I asked once, a Russian. I said, why do you have a gun, that, therefore-- he says to me, if I have a gun, I have bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to go around when the Russians go home. Then the Poles took over. Jews didn't go for  the guns. They didn't have it in them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the reason, I'm trying to tell you, they were afraid of us. They didn't know, actually, who we were. A German did not know  a Jew from a non-Jew. And how long did you stay in this DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed about several months. And then we said, let's travel Germany. Maybe we'll find somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? We found out there's women camps. There's men camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled and traveled and traveled, maybe four or five months, from one day to another day. We didn't know where to go. But we traveled. And then we came back. And some of our friends went to a city of Marburg. Marburg was a depot from American GIs, whoever that came in and then quarantined, from there they send them out to Germany. It was part of Germany. And several of our boys worked in [? the ?] [? PX. ?] And-- and the others, they all worked in American institutions. There was food. I mean, whatever we needed. We didn't need, I think, much clothes. There was no clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was 17. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And me and my three friends took off to Marburg. The war was, in those days-- in fact, Nazi had several rooms. And survivors came in, a room was taken from them. They had to give up something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I and one friend went to one room. And another two friends went to-- they were further, in another room. And I got a job working for the PX.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had everything. Food was no question, really. They threw away more than somebody else had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Americans treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Well, I had all the clothes in the world. I wore American uniform. I didn't have any insignias. I couldn't have any insignias. But treated very well. I met some people who spoke Polish. And the fact is that I worked very close with them, with the officers. But it was all temporary. We didn't know what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any Jewish Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Quite a few. In fact, those Friday nights, they used to come up. They brought food for us. And Rita-- which is my wife now. And at that time, I only know her-- I was going with her as a girlfriend-- did-- and another two girls did the cooking. We met Friday night, in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I decide to get married? I-- we left Marburg. And we went to Frankfurt, at a DP camp, where all our friends were. This thing was coming to an end, with the PX business. And in those days, not like today, we couldn't sleep with the women here, in the same room. It was difficult. So I met a friend, which was a [? sonderkommando, ?] which died in Israel. His name was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --Morris Friedman. Was he with you at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in [? the ?] [? sonderkommando. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just met him coincidentally. And he said to me, what are you doing here? I says, I'm not doing anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no place, where to go to. So he invited me, with my present-- my present wife, to come to Stuttgart. And he knew people there, that we could get, at least, a place where to stay. And that's the way it was. Meanwhile, he also met a girl in-- in that DP camp, Rita's friend. So she went with Rita. And I went with him. And several months later, we decided to get married. So he got married a few weeks before me. And I got married with Rita, in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A very Orthodox wedding. Because in those days, the rabbis, the survivors were most from Hungary. And they were very, very Orthodox rabbis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a wedding. It was a hundred people. We didn't have much food. But we had a good time. Each one of the friends baked something, a cake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a-- a place where they sold beer. So we had beer, maybe a little whiskey too. And we had a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart, we stayed from '47 to '49. We stayed almost three years. And we didn't plan to have a child. We didn't know what's going to happen to us. And me, I-- I skipped that when I was in Marburg, I met a Jewish GI from Brooklyn. And I told him that I have family in the-- in the United States. And he actually found a cousin, a first cousin, for me. I wrote a letter to them and explained to them where I am and what happened. They couldn't make it out. They were elderly people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they kept asking me, what are you doing in Germany? You were born in Poland. Would you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, I told them, in a letter-- I couldn't write Jewish. They were reading Jewish. Rita wrote Jewish very good. Because I didn't have that kind of education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told her to write that I met a girl and I think I'm going to get married. They didn't understand that. And they said, why do you want to get married there? There's plenty girls here. They didn't want me to get married. Oh. We will pause now, to change the tape.
